docs.title: Bana
docs.text: A yak-like creature often used by the Rurii as a beast of burden. It lives on hills and mountains in the Karrow and eastern Osera.

docs.title: Echoes
docs.text:
Echoes are minor spirits believed to exist in all living things, though are particularly strong in specific people under certain conditions. They are considered mirrors of the soul, though can have their own personality quirks. They are referred to as 'Daemons' by scholars of the Seventh Sanctum, 'Fravash' by Rurii shamans, 'Nim(s)' by Brethiln druids.

It is believed that Echoes can be animated to perform magic through various ways of interaction, including (but not limited to): Arcanum, Phasonomy, Geomancery, meditation, hallucinogenic episodes (substance or experience-induced), or, in rare cases, by simply conversing with oneself (if the Echo is receptive/strong enough).

It is believed by certain scholars that Lethe have no Echoes, and are instead made from the same substance, whatever that might be.

docs.title: Horrors
docs.text: Horrors are many-legged, blind or many-eyed creatures that live in the Haze. Their white, leathery skin acts as near-perfect camouflage within the misty conditions that they inhabit, though should they find themselves outside of their preferred environment they are easily spotted.

docs.title: Origin
docs.text: It is unknown where exactly horrors originate. Their appearance on both Osera and Hespenor suggests that they likely existed on Halcyon before it was torn in twain. Their as-of-yet unrecorded presence on Zinnabar further suggests that they are a phenomenon restricted by geography.

docs.title: Biology
docs.text: The sages of the Seventh Sanctum have studied horrors for centuries but have very little to show for it: Captured horrors quickly 'wilt' without access to fresh Mist, which they seem to 'breathe' through their skin. It is assumed that horrors require the mist to survive, as they often become catatonic outside of it, though the precise time it takes for a horror to achieve this state varies. A horror has never been seen to reproduce, and besides their limbs do not seem to have appendages suitable for reproduction.

docs.title: Variants
docs.text: Some horrors: - have more limbs than others, though the usual number is six. - are many-eyed, though many more seem entirely blind. - have a carapace-like armoured hide, able to resist spear-thrusts and arrows - are bulbous in appearance, and are likely egg-hatching (though this has not been observed) - have a human-like silhouette attached to a longer, worm-like body, which they use to lure unsuspecting and helpful people further into the mist, or into pits - have scythe-like appendages on their forelimbs, able to cut into heavy armour

docs.title: Meta
docs.text:
A wolf-horse-like beast that inhabits the Karrow. The Rurii tame these beasts as mounts and as hunting companions.

From the Seventh Sanctum's catalogue of the beasts of the eastern wastes, compiled in the 141st year of the Cenerian Age. Where the Sanctum has not seen a thing with its own eyes, it has set down what is reported, and named the reporting for what it is.

The beast that the men of the League call the karrowhound is neither hound nor horse, though it borrows the figure of both, and the elder catalogues are right to warn that the name misleads the unwary. It is a creature of the Karrow, that Haze-steeped waste east of Osera, and it is found nowhere that the Haze does not lie low upon the ground. The Rurii, who alone have mastered it, do not call it by any name that we can render; the Great Morr of their oral telling is said to have ridden one out of the wastes, and from this all their riding is descended.

docs.title: Of its form
docs.text:
In bulk it is as a courser, broad enough across the back to bear a grown rider, yet it carries the long jaw and the lean gait of a wolf, so that those who meet it for the first time take it for a monster stitched of two animals. Its feet are neither the true hoof of the horse nor the soft pad of the hound, but a splayed and horned foot between the two, which serves it equally upon the hard steppe and the scrambling stone of the mesas. Its coat is a grizzled grey that beads the damp of the Haze and sheds it, and that renders it near invisible in the mist, in the manner of the Horrors, though for the opposite purpose: the Horror hides to wait, the karrowhound hides to run down.

Most striking is that the karrowhound sees little and needs to see less. Its eyes are clouded and small, and the authorities agree it hunts by scent and by the tremor of the ground, reading the passage of prey through the hard earth as a man reads a road by daylight. In this it is fitted to its country, for in the perpetual Haze of the Karrow the eye is the least useful of the senses, and a beast that trusted to sight would starve.

docs.title: Of its habits and its taming
docs.text: It runs in small bonded bands and does not bay as a dog bays, but utters a low call that carries far through the mist. The Rurii take the young from the den and rear them from the first, and it is reported that a Ruri and his hound are paired for the one life, hunting together and, in the wars of migration, going to battle together. When Xeril's conquests drove that people west into Ceneria and Zinnabar, the karrowhounds went with them; and here is a thing worth the traveller's knowing, for it answers an old confusion. The Horror, taken from the Haze, wilts and dies for want of the Mist it breathes. The karrowhound does not. It breeds, hunts, and grows fat far from the Karrow, which is proof enough that it is a true beast of flesh and not, whatever some hold, a Horror grown tame.

docs.title: Of differing accounts
docs.text: Some sages will have it that the karrowhound is no more than a Horror that the Rurii have bent to use, and they point to the shared coat and the shared blindness as their warrant. The Sanctum does not hold with this. The Horror breathes the mist and perishes without it; the karrowhound eats flesh and thrives in the green lands; the two breed each after its own kind and never the one into the other. That they wear the same grey is the work of the same country upon two different beasts, as two unrelated men grow brown under the one sun. The Rurii themselves laugh at the question, and say only that the hound was given to their first Great Morr in the wastes, which the Sanctum records as their telling and not as its own.

docs.title: The lesson of the karrowhound
docs.text: And here the older catalogues append a gloss, which we keep for its use to the preacher. The karrowhound goes near blind through a country where all is hidden, and yet it never loses the trail, for it does not trust the eye but the surer senses beneath. So too, they say, is the faithful soul that walks through a clouded world: it is not the one who sees furthest that holds the truest course, but the one who keeps the scent of the thing it loves. And let no man despise the beast for its ill-made look, for the creature taken for a monster has proved, in the hand of the Rurii, the most faithful of all companions.

docs.title: Lethe
docs.text:
Sometimes called shapeshifters, shifters, hidden people, or Elves, the Lethe are mysterious beings which are feared by all humans in Osera. They only inhabit deep woods and forests, and are known to kill intruders, especially those with ill intent.

Though rare, they have been observed to steal young children and replace them with one of their own, having taken the child's face and identity. Why this is done is unknown, but has resulted in retaliatory attacks into Lethe groves.

The Lethe are rumoured to be the cause of the Haze and for breeding Horrors, though this is contested by the scholars of the Seventh Sanctum who suggest the two are separate phenomena.

docs.title: Rykes
docs.text:
Rykes are extremely large raptors that stalk the skies of the Karrow. They are the symbol of Thalendor and its Aldrician dynasty ever since they adopted them as hunting birds during their great migration from the Unsung Lands.

A Ryke has a wingspan of 4 metres, and usually weighs the same as a small hunting hound. They are infamous for attacking and carrying off small livestock, pets, and even children.

Rykes are, however, marginally intelligent animals, able to be trained but not thoroughly domesticated. They are wilful and headstrong but can understand that cooperating with their handler will net them stable access to food.

Those who keep Rykes are known as Rykers, and are often trusted individuals in Thalen courts.

docs.title: Skrats
docs.text:
Skrats are swamp and bog dwelling humanoids, not much larger than a child. They are, however, extremely strong for their size, on par with a late teenager, and can inflict serious wounds on unsuspecting travellers and undefended hamlets.

Snivelling, feral, mindless, anarchic, and uncivilised are regular words used for Skrats. Nevertheless, Skrats do have a very (very) basic level of culture, mostly involving the survival of their inhospitable living conditions through invading, looting, and killing their surroundings, or each other.

docs.title: Titans
docs.text:
Mythological beings rumoured to be the creators of the bridges that connect much of Oseran islands and peninsulas. Scholars of the Seventh Sanctum have theorised that the Titans, if they existed, were likely afraid of water or had no ability to swim or fashion boats.

The Rurii have an oral history which claims that they are the descendants of runaway slaves of these Titans. In their stories, the Titans lived on top of the mountains that dot Osera.

docs.title: Wardens
docs.text:
Wardens are beings of stone resembling armoured men in ancient style, presumed to be Mycean battle dress. Their origin is unknown, though most attribute them to the Myceans as some kind of unliving soldiers.

Wardens range from active to catatonic. Active Wardens roam the countryside in unknowable fashion, some moving in straight lines (even through homes and over mountains) until they can move no longer. Others patrol a certain area, slaying all who step into it with their swords made of Heavenly Iron. Yet more simply kneel or stand in place, moving only under certain conditions, each as unique as the Warden itself.

docs.title: Anzo
docs.text: Cenerian God (with human origin) of war, deception, and violent death, represented by a dagger. It is taboo to worship Anzo in Cenerian culture, though in moments before battle it is common for soldiers to pray to him to kill their enemies.

docs.title: Bel
docs.text:
Primeval deity of darkness, water, night, and non-violent death, represented by a fish. The worship of Bel is popular among Cenerian fishermen and sailors, who sacrifice to it out of fear.

Bel is said to rule the Underworld, which is often thought of as underneath the ocean in Mycean mythology. His rule is connected to it via the Pillars of Bel, which are said to be the direct path between the Heavens, the mortal world and the Underworld.

docs.title: Luminor
docs.text: Primeval deity of light, fire, life, and truth, represented by the sun. Worshipped by the Sect of Illumination and is a core part of Cenerian beliefs.

docs.title: Melos
docs.text: The 'dark' aspect of Luminor, ever since the Collapse, Melos is no longer worshipped. Some Mycean cults of his were known to have existed, however. They said that Melos was the dark side of the sun, never seen, but just as powerful.

docs.title: Mor
docs.text:
Mor is death personified, worshipped mostly by the citizens of Karsolam in Hespenor and by the Order of the Pale Maiden, who quest across the Lands of Mor to guide souls to the Underworld, Mor's domain.

Mor is not part of the original Mycean pantheon, and it is unclear when worship of him started. In the original Mycean mythology, the Underworld is ruled by Bel.

docs.title: Pyos
docs.text:
God (with human origin) of oaths, laws, and civilisation, represented by an eye. Worshipped by the Sect of Pyos. Originally worshipped by Myceans, then Cenerians, and now popular among the Thalen, who initially called him Tioric but have begun adopting the Cenerian word (though often pronounce it as Pjoss).

Pyos is believed to have founded the ancient city of Mycea and ensuring its dominance by giving its people the knowledge of Arcanum.

docs.title: Sky Mother
docs.text: A Rurii and Brethiln deity who rules the heavens. The Brethiln believe that the Sky-Mother is the wife of the World-Father, whose existence is not known among Rurii worship.

docs.title: Verdhas
docs.text:
An old aspect of Luminor, once worshipped by Mycean soldiers and officers. Verdhas was worshipped by slaying a bull and subsequent feasting, a reference to his own mythological bull slaying. Worship of Verdhas was somewhat taboo, as it presupposed that Luminor was not a single deity.

Verdhas is said to be the aspect of fire and holy war. Worship of him as has been revived with Andros, who has been proclaimed 'Verdhas reborn' by his own priests.

docs.title: World Father
docs.text: Brethiln deity that presides over all land, life, believed to be at home in the mythical 'World Tree' the location of which has been lost to time. In Brethiln lore, the 'Sky-Mother' is his wife, and all beings are their children. Interestingly, the Rurii also worship a being they call the Sky-Mother.

docs.title: Alvos
docs.text:
The last sovereign of Mycea and the First League, after whom the shallow Sea of Alvos is named.

Andros of the Cenerian League claims direct descent from Alvos, though this is disputed.

docs.title: Andros
docs.text:
Andros is the Archon of Pallas and Sovereign of the Cenerian League, though he claims to be Sovereign of 'all known lands'. He is Cenerian born and claims dynastic lineage to Mycean nobility, most famously Alvos himself.

The Crowning of Andros was the confluence of his political power, happening when he was 24 years old as a response to the Voyage of Normund and Xeril's Conquests.

Andros has succeeded in signing a peace treaty with Normund and has dedicated the years since his coronation to defeating Xeril in south-eastern Ceneria and Zinnabar with a combined force of Cenerian soldiers and Altani mercenaries. This is known as the War of Recovery.

During the war, Andros has made a name for himself as the 'Celestial Lion', the 'Son of Dawn', and the 'Bull Slayer' - a reference to Verdhas, an aspect of Luminor who is no longer worshipped but has been revived by Andros' Seer Therem to rouse religious fervour for a god of war that isn't Anzo.

docs.title: Azuth
docs.text: The Archmage founder of the Seventh Sanctum and namesake of the Sea of Azuth. Azuth was one of the few fifth degree Arcanum wielders who did not appreciably go insane. He was known to have lived a long life, longer than most. He died in an expedition to the Barrier Islands.

docs.title: Normund
docs.text: Normund is the son of the great Thalen king Aldric who led his kindred across the Karrow from the Unsung Lands and into contemporary Brethil, now Thalendor. He is the current king of Thalendor, who made his name with his invasion of Ceneria, known as the Voyage of Normund.

docs.title: Pale Maiden
docs.text: The Pale Maiden is a prophetess of death, allegedly guiding lost souls through the Lands of Mor to the gates of the Underworld where they are to face judgement by Mor's judges. She is said to be an elusive figure, and all those who lay eyes on her are compelled to join her in her task as the Order of the Pale Maiden.

docs.title: Xeril
docs.text:
A Rurii warlord who conquered much of south-eastern Ceneria and Zinnabar, known as Xeril's Conquests.

He currently resides in Zeutana, which he has made his capital. He is engaged in an on-going war with Andros in the War of Recovery.

docs.title: Akkurim
docs.text: The Akkurim are an #extinct culture that used to inhabit the eastern parts of Halcyon, long before it became Osera. They were one of the great challengers of the First League, being renowned for their chariots and pike formations. They were smaller people than the Myceans, though not by much.

docs.title: Meta
docs.text:
• Responsible for Arcanum in its earliest form, later adopted by Myceans
  • Worshipped a God of truth and order known as Ennu, and an 'evil deity' of war and murder as counterweight known as Anzim. (Pyos, Anzo)
  • Their homeland, now the Karrow, was made uninhabitable. The Myceans developed Haze to 'flush' their enemies out and 'salt' their lands.

docs.title: Altani
docs.text:
The Altani are giants inhabiting many mountain ranges across Osera. They often stand at seven or eight feet tall, and have strong builds due to a diet of meat and milk. They are androgynous, and only reproduce once in an individual's lifetime. Their skins range from swarthy to grey to pale.

"It is curious that the beings we call Altani are called 'Children of the Titans' in Rurii speak. They seem to have accepted that the Altani, to them at least, are the descendants of the fabled bridge builders."

docs.title: Brethiln
docs.text:
The Brethiln are the original inhabitants of Osera alongside the Ykrittar. They are great carpenters and hunters, having made the many woods of the continent their source of wealth and sustenance.

Male Names: Arlekh, Belgir, Borun, Bracho, Branoc, Bredan, Cadfin, Col, Crahan, Dermot, Doru, Druli, Fenegan, Gasti, Gawen, Granyd, Lago, Lum, Luichan, Lyd, Merag, Nal, Rath, Rugan, Sein, Udris, Ydwin

Female Names: Abeil, Anwyn, Beitrin, Brigis, Cidre, Eilidh, Eira, Gweth, Imulir, Kara, Leadh, Moera, Nia, Norena, Olawyn, Rhia, Selag, Una, Ylen

docs.title: Appearance
docs.text: Brethiln range from short to tall with great variance. Those living in the hills are shorter, while the Brethiln of the coast and woods are taller, subsisting of a diet of meat and fish. Their skin is rather pale, though their hair is dark, often turning grey and pure white at earlier ages than other cultures. Their clothing is usually practical.

docs.title: Origin
docs.text: The Brethiln are believed to be the ancestral inhabitants of Osera. Their own stories and folklore speaks of the World-Father carving the first Brethiln from the trees.

docs.title: Culture
docs.text: Brethiln are pastoralists with some limited agriculture, mostly dedicated to the eventual production of alcohol. They are a gregarious people, holding many feasts (especially after a bountiful hunt) that are celebrated with the wider village or town. Brethiln do not have cities as such, and the largest equivalent are fortress towns home to some hundreds of people but no more.

docs.title: Beliefs
docs.text: The Brethiln believe in 'World-Father' as a primary deity, attributing all life and existence to him. The Brethiln believe that he made all beings, and that he will judge them collectively in the end-times. Some Brethiln believe the Collapse were those end-times, and that any who survived are now doomed to wander the world, even after death. In their view, the World-Father judged the Brethiln poorly, and their recent setbacks as a people to the Thalen conquests is proof of this.

docs.title: Politics
docs.text: The Brethiln operate as a decentralised clan system across many hearths and larger villages. Each household has a sworn fealty to a hearth, and each hearth has fealty to a clan. The clans in turn elect their most senior chieftains who form the Moot (alongside the Druids), which in turn can elect a Great Chieftain. During the Thalen invasion, the Great Chieftain Rogan was elected, who promised to turn the fortunes of the Brethiln people. He died at the Battle of Oathbridge.

docs.title: Cenerian
docs.text:
Cenerians are a proud urban & seafaring culture that traces its lineage back to Mycean days. Many Cenerian cities are politically allied via the Cenerian League, including some on Zinnabar, which has a closely aligned but somewhat distinct culture colloquially known as Southern.

Male Names: Acarion, Achios, Acton, Altenos, Amnon, Andros, Arion, Castor, Crotor, Danos, Denos, Destor, Darion, Encurion, Galon, Gavalon, Hecaron, Lacalion, Leosios, Lucon, Lycon, Lycos, Manteos, Menon, Milos, Nemos, Nethor, Nicasor, Obron, Ocheros, Oros, Pelicos, Pharon, Poryclos, Rhanos, Sachanion, Semnon, Serandon, Sovos, Syneos, Taros, Tasynor, Temion, Varios, Velanon, Zenon

Female Names: Achena, Adinea, Alena, Anea, Ariada, Arytha, Catella, Chara, Corena, Danira, Donysa, Elea, Emyna, Eodisia, Gala, Halea, Halcya, Helea, Hedena, Hyradia, Ira, Icala, Iolanna, Laria, Lysica, Mavea, Mina, Mitara, Nadea, Panalea, Phaea, Popilia, Rhamira, Rhoe, Salea, Sora, Synira, Temera, Thylea, Valaria, Viria, Viviana, Zena, Zimena

docs.title: Appearance
docs.text: Cenerians are average height people, often having a swarthier appearance than their Brethiln or Thalen neighbours, especially those who live on Zinnabar. Shorter hair is considered virtuous among males, especially the nobility, and females often grow their hair out but are expected to cover it, the covering's fabric revealing their social status. Colourful cloth is highly appreciated, particularly in the shades of the sea.

docs.title: Origin
docs.text: Cenerians are named after the southern region of Osera initially named by the Mycean seafaring colonisers. In time, these colonies began to self-identify among themselves and others as Cenerian, a name and people that survived the Collapse.

docs.title: Culture
docs.text:
Cenerians have inhabited much from their Mycean origin. They have continued the urban traditions upon which their initial colonisation efforts rested. They have also continued the seafaring traditions of their ancestors, and every city in their culture is linked by river, coast, or sea routes.

Cenerians have three cultural norms that were passed down from Mycean days:

  • Honour - personal respect that is commanded by an individual, but also a term used when referring to the value of a hostage or slave.
  • Renown - A post-death equivalent of honour, which is determined by how well one dies. Less important in Ceneria, highly valued by Southerners.
  • Nemesis - Anyone committing acts of hubris, shame, or disrespect welcome nemesis as a response to their social violations, which in extreme circumstances can mean exile or that one's life is forfeit to whoever chooses to take it, in essence, an outlaw.

docs.title: Beliefs
docs.text: Cenerians believe in four primary deities, two of which are considered 'good' and 'bad' respectively. Minor deities, 'Daemons' exist in all living things according to Cenerians and are referred to as 'Echoes' of the soul. The primary deities are further split in two: one 'primeval' deity and one 'human' deity, representing the grander beginnings before humanity and humanity's ascension/fall respectively.

docs.title: Deities
docs.text: Listed in order of 'acceptability of worship' in Cenerian society: - Luminor - primeval deity of light, fire, life, and truth, represented by the sun. Worshipped by the Sect of Illumination - Pyos - human god of oaths, laws, and civilisation, represented by an eye. Worshipped by the Sect of Pyos. - Bel - primeval deity of darkness, water, night, and non-violent death, represented by a fish. - Anzo - human god of war, deception, and violent death, represented by a dagger. The Sigodo infamously worship Anzo outrightly.

docs.title: Politics
docs.text:
Cenerians have strong political foundations. Their society is organised along urban foundations, each city making up its own polity. Though variations exist, most are headed by an 'Archon' and a 'Vizier' who are elected from among the most powerful families who have proven their virtue (done through family records, general social gravitas, leading defences or navies, and through investing funds into public works). These elections can be for life, though this is uncommon. Most Archon-Vizier duos are voted out when they fall out of favour, if they are not assassinated first.

The cities are linked via the Cenerian League, founded in the aftermath of the Collapse by the Archon of Pallas and Enetia as a union of northern and southern Ceneria.

The exception to this is the far city of Karsolam which rules itself under different conditions while nominally remaining part of the wider Cenerian world.

Recently, Andros, Archon of Pallas has revived the old title of 'Sovereign', an event known as the Crowning of Andros.

docs.title: Society
docs.text:
Cenerian society is socially fluid among its free citizens, but not its slave caste. A rough outline:

  • Seravon - the ruling elite who own farmland and are called to fight as heavy cavalry. This elite is further divided by offices, such as Archons, Councillors, and Kephars who rule over fortresses, towns, and important villages.
  • Citizens - anyone living within the walls of a city, pays tax, and provides military service.
  • Freemen - free villagers, farmers, artisans not living within cities. Same rights as citizens but not allowed to live within the city
  • Parasts - military slaves that are exempt from manual labour as long as they fight
  • Slaves - hereditary slaves from the first peoples conquered by the Myceans

docs.title: Manlings
docs.text: The Manlings are a shorter breed of humans, primarily found in Bal-Gorund, their main city. They are sometimes, derogatorily, referred to as 'Dwarves' or 'Halflings'. They are known for their underground structures which tunnel through the largest peak of the Akkron Wall, Mount Watcher. They are incredibly secretive, and no living outsiders are known to have entered Bal-Gorund.

docs.title: Origin
docs.text: It is unclear whether Manlings predate or derive from humans. There are next to no sources mentioning 'Manlings' specifically, making research about them next to impossible. Their own sages are not forthcoming with information, either.

docs.title: Interactions with other cultures
docs.text: It has become a fashion in Ceneria to have at least one Manling on an Archon's council, both for their insights but also their uncanny ability in Arcanum. The kings and barons of Thalendor have adopted this system as well. Sometimes these Manlings are employed when rare trading convoys reach these rulers, other times they forcibly recruited.

docs.title: Mycean
docs.text:
The original but now #extinct human culture of Halcyon which dominated the continent. Artefacts and ruins survive their rule, though many lie at the bottom of the Sea of Alvos, including the capital, Mycea.

Mycea itself was the jewel of the First League and its de facto leading polity. The Archons of Mycea styled themselves 'Sovereigns' of the League, and were rarely challenged over the eight hundred years of the League's existence.

The Myceans are often rumoured to have been responsible for creating the Wardens, but this remains conjecture.

docs.title: Qedhi
docs.text:
The Qedhi are a scattered people, whose diaspora live in many of the originally Mycean, now Cenerian, cities. They were one of the many conquered peoples subsumed by the First League and proved adaptable enough to the Mycean way of life to inhabit their own quarters within the city walls of the various colonies.

The Qedhi relationship with their conquerors was not always harmonious. Over the centuries slave uprisings, xenophobia, and misunderstandings often boiled over. In some cities, the entire Qedhi population was murdered, in others they fled.

docs.title: Rurii
docs.text: The Rurii are a people that originate from the Karrow, a land east of Osera and the wastes that separate it from the Unsung Lands. After Xeril's Conquests, many permanently migrated into the more fertile regions of Ceneria and Zinnabar. They refer to themselves as the Mora-Bantár, and their leaders as Great Morr.

docs.title: Appearance
docs.text: Rurii are considered not only a culture but a race apart from other Oserans. They differ quite significantly from other races, particularly in their backward knees and fur-like covering of their bodies, which are hunched.

docs.title: Origin
docs.text: The Rurii keep an oral history of their people which states they are the descendants of the runaway slaves of the Titans. Whatever their origin, their stay in the Karrow has deeply affected them: they are hairier than most, and are often considered 'beastlike' by man other cultures.

docs.title: Culture
docs.text: The Rurii have a deep passion for stories and song, using a variety of sounds, clicks, and throat vibrations to bring their tales to life. While they are considered extremely warlike, their culture also has a deep respect for peace (after violent means have been exhausted to save face). They are also renowned beast tamers, having tamed the yak-like Bana of the Karrow which they inhabit, as well as the wolf-horse-like Karrowhound.

docs.title: Beliefs
docs.text: The Rurii's primary deity is the Sky-Mother, though they also believe in the spirits of all living things. They have many minor gods, most who are not known to outsiders. They also perform sacrifices to the Titans, whom they continue to fear.

docs.title: Relations
docs.text: Other cultures of Osera have a dim view of the Rurii. They often consider them 'beastly', both in appearance and manners. They are, however, respected for their prowess and survivability, which are undeniable to even the staunchest of detractors.

docs.title: Southern
docs.text:
A subset of the Cenerian culture which has evolved alongside it after the fall of Mycea. 'Southern' is the (sometimes derogatory) term used by Cenerians across the Sea of Azuth for their southern kin. Southerners usually refer to themselves as Cenerian and sometimes still as Mycean.

It is unclear where the exact cultural fault lines between Cenerian and Southern lie exactly. Depending on who is asked, Southerners only come from Zinnabar. Others include islanders in the Sea of Azuth as part of the wider Southern sub-culture, particularly because these islanders also often insist on their Mycean heritage.

Southerners are known for their fanatic reverence of duty and family honour, their hygiene, and their assassins, most famous of which are the Sigodo.

docs.title: Thalen
docs.text:
Migrants from the Unsung Lands, the Thalen are master horsemen: lance and stirrup were both perfected under their hand. The harsh eastern threats of their homeland made fortifiers of them too, and the castle-building knowledge they carried into Osera gave them a sharp edge over the Brethiln on whose lands Thalendor was founded.

Male Names: Alay, Alaren, Athegar, Amalric, Anbard, Baldulf, Borvald, Brunn, Chalric, Cunbert, Dalard, Durold, Ecard, Erdurand, Folc, Funbard, Galrec, Galthard, Gilvard, Gunteric, Hecard, Harobert, Inric, Lomarc, Lorgand, Merovic, Norbund, Olither, Osric, Perin, Romund, Rothard, Servic, Sigard, Sorgar, Thalric, Thalvin, Tharerd, Theodric, Ulvard, Varmund, Vigother

Female Names: Adalis, Asela, Beltrud, Beswinda, Brunda, Catalida, Carmenet, Daliset, Elta, Elys, Eywyn, Friduna, Gerta, Gisilind, Iliset, Isigund, Liena, Lis, Melind, Mitela, Roslind, Saraset, Sigalind, Silvind, Thalva, Theuda, Vilatrud

docs.title: Appearance
docs.text: Thalen are relatively tall and broad-shouldered people. Males wear short-cropped beards (long unkempt ones are considered dirty) but both males and females keep longer hair, often 'done-up' in a bun, hair-do, or braids. Short hair is considered childish, but long, flowing hair as befitting only those who do not perform labour, such as noble women.

docs.title: Origin
docs.text: The Thalen keep stories of their migrations from the Unsung Lands, a once thriving land that turned foul with rot over time, forcing them west. The Thalen were only one of many cultures living there, but no others have attempted the crossing of the wastes between those lands and Osera.

docs.title: Culture
docs.text: The Thalen are organised around petty and princely households. Everything within their society is done as a household unit, from business to agriculture to warfare. To maintain diversity and cultural connection, adoption of wards is commonplace, though not on the permanent level as the Cenerians. Thalens value sturdy estates and castles, as well as good horsemanship and the rearing of powerful war horses. They are entirely reliant on agriculture to feed their population.

docs.title: Beliefs
docs.text: Thalen have had an ancestor-worshipping religion as far as they remember. Ever since their arrival in Osera, however, many have taken a liking to Cenerian culture, including their beliefs. In particular, Pyos has been adopted as a primary god among many Thalen, who have flocked to join the Sect of Pyos in Movenna. This is mostly attributed to Aldric's conversion before winning the Battle of Oathbridge, which resulted in many of his followers converting too. Nevertheless, ancestor worship remains ubiquitous in Thalen culture, and often happens alongside worship of Pyos.

docs.title: Politics
docs.text: The Aldrician dynasty has ruled Thalendor since its inception, its foundation built on its vassals who rule from imposing castles. Each Thalen castellan owes personal loyalty and tax from their fields to the monarch, who in turn supports each castellan against natural and unnatural threats through their combined might.

docs.title: Ykrittar
docs.text:
The Ykrittar are a secluded and isolated people of the north. They have little contact with the rest of Osera but are relatively well-known due to their regular appearance as boogeymen in children's tales.

"If you don't eat your soup, the men of Ykrittar will come and take you away to the far icy wastes of the North!"

docs.title: 1. Timeline of Events
docs.text: Erona has had, according to Mycean reckoning, three distinct ages, each of which began with a calamity that befell the world. The first age, the Fabled Age, has no real timeline of events, being steeped in myth and legend. It ended in the God-War, a fabled battle where two human demi-gods and two primordial gods (Pyos, Luminor, Bel, Anzo) rose against the other gods for supremacy of the heavens, and won, leaving the world to mortals.

docs.title: The Mycean Age
docs.text: Year Event 1 M.A. The mythical founding of Mycea, supposedly by Pyos after the God-War as a departing gift to mortals ~150 M.A. Founding of the First League as a check against a growing, rival league, the two locked into bitter conflict for decades, followed by peace, only to be followed by war again. ~600 M.A. Mycean colonisation of eastern Halcyon begins by sea, eventually evolving into Ceneria. The first wars with the Akkurim begin. ~750-~1250 M.A. Peak Mycean dominance after uniting most of Halcyon under the First League against outside threats. Heavenly Iron forging begins, Arcanum is codified in the First Sanctum. Mycean powder is invented. Haze first appears. 1449 M.A. The Collapse. Earthquakes, floods, eruptions, asteroid strikes. Halcyon splits in two: Osera in the east, Hespenor in the west. Mycea destroyed; most First League cities destroyed. Karsolam alone survives in Hesperon, isolated. The Mycean culture, as a continuous polity, ends.

docs.title: The Dark Age
docs.text: Year Event 1 D.A. - 81 D.A. Erona reels under the effects of the Collapse. Survivors regroup on Osera, now the eastern half of former Halcyon, which was least affected by the flood due to its rugged landscape. The days are dark and grey, and soot falls from the sky, earning these 80 odd years the nomer of Dark Age. The 6th Sanctum is founded and burned as scapegoats for the Collapse.

docs.title: The Cenerian Age
docs.text: Year Event 1 C.A. Cenerian League founded by the Archon of Enetia and Pallas as a union of northern and southern Ceneria against threats both natural and unnatural. 11 C.A. The Seventh Sanctum is founded and given formal protections by Pallas. 79 C.A. Karsolam re-establishes contact with the east, nominally joins the Cenerian League for trade. 112 C.A. Thalen Migration begins under king Aldric, fleeing the rotting Unsung Lands and crossing Karrow into Osera until they reach the sea, conquering Ostellyth. 114 C.A. First battles between Brethiln and Thalen begin. Rogan is elected Great Chieftain of all Brethil clans by the Moot to resist the invaders. 118 C.A. The Battle of Oathbridge ends in Thalen victory, resulting in the formation of the kingdom of Thalendor in the southern half of Brethil. Aldric and most of his nobles convert to the Sect of Pyos before the battle. Aldric dies of his wounds shortly after. 121 C.A. Normund is crowned king of Thalendor after short infighting between Aldric's children known as the Sons' War. 129-140 C.A. Voyage of Normund. Normund's armies attack and plunder much of Ceneria, ending only after the Cenerian League pays tribute and allows Thalen priests the same rights as Cenerian priests in Movenna. 138-147 C.A. Xeril's Conquests. Rurii warbands cross out of the Karrow and into southeastern Ceneria. Thenea Deuteron and Zeutana fall, Naporia resists. 139 C.A. Crowning of Andros. With the league beset on all sides, Andros is crowned as 'Sovereign' and given command of the united armies of the cities. 141 C.A. Mycean powder is alchemised by the Seventh Sanctum. 155 C.A. Present Day.

docs.title: Battle of Oathbridge
docs.text:
The Battle of Oathbridge was a decisive battle between the clans of the Brethiln led by High Chief Rogan and the army led by the Thalen king Aldric. The result was an overwhelming victory for the Thalen, who used the battle and subsequent rout of the Brethiln to demarcate their northern borders of Thalendor.

It was before and during this battle that Aldric converted to the Sect of Pyos, after supposedly seeing the sign (an eye) of Pyos in the sky illuminating the Brethiln armies, allowing him to predict their movement and counter it. Aldric died soon after but his conversion was recorded by his high advisor and personal priest. The battle was fought at an (to the Thalen) unnamed bridge, which they henceforth named Oathbridge, to honour Aldric's oath to the god of truth.

Some Thalen disputed that Aldric ever converted, blaming a biased history written by a Pyosian priest who used the events of the battle in favour of the Sect of Pyos. Nevertheless, many Thalen nobles followed suit in converting, including Aldric's eventual successor, Normund.

docs.title: Collapse
docs.text:
A cataclysmic event of earthquakes, volcanic eruptions, falling stars, and an eventual flood that shook Erona. It split the original continent of Halcyon in twain, leaving behind Osera and Hespenor.

Except for the largest peaks, most of Halcyon was drowned during the flood. Some sages predicted this collapse, and the second, third, and fourth Sanctums were moved to these peaks, now known as the Barrier Islands.

docs.title: Crowning of Andros
docs.text:
The crowning of Andros was a momentous occasion which marked the ascent of Andros as 'sovereign', a title not used since Mycean days.

Andros used the crowning, attended by nearly all Archons of the Cenerian League, to unite them under his banner.

The crowning was a reaction to the dual threat of the Voyage of Normund and Xeril's Conquests, which threatened Cenerian existence.

It is rumoured that Andros used the Sigodo's reputation to coerce some Archons into supporting his coronation during the Crowning of Andros.

docs.title: Sons' War
docs.text:
A succession crisis and civil war between the children of Aldric which resulted in the victory of Normund, who was crowned shortly after.

Normund was very young at the time, and won thanks to the aid of the great Thalen commander and castellan Ecard.

docs.title: Voyage of Normund
docs.text:
Normund is the son of the great Thalen king Aldric who led his kindred across the Karrow from the Unsung Lands and into contemporary Brethil, now Thalendor.

After the succession crisis of the Sons' War, Normund became king and consolidated his power. After internal challenges to his rule rose again, he used the crisis to launch a campaign against Cenerian cities, first in the north and eventually into the south.

The invasions were only possible due to the large Cenerian fleet left behind during the Thalen conquest of Ostellyth, but Normund and his armies surprised all by their quick mastery of the waves. Instead of using Cenerian ramming tactics, they instead perfect the art of ship-to-ship boarding, using their superior men-at-arms at close quarters, supported by close range crossbow fire.

docs.title: Campaign
docs.text:
Normund began his campaign by pre-empting a second succession crisis and sending his topmost rivals to besiege northern Cenerian cities such as Koray and Enetia. Koray was quickly conquered, but a protracted siege at Enetia resulted in Normund sending a second wave towards the south, leaving a minimal force to continue the siege,

The second wave found success at Essalon on the island of Gorgos, which was used as a forward base for the remainder of the campaign and continues to be ruled by a Thalen vassal. Normund was successfully turned away at Asparon, but sacked Varanth, Tanura, and Oneos, razing the latter two to the ground after weeks of looting.

Only when Normund's armies began threatening Movenna and Pallas, and when Xeril's Conquests began in tandem, did the Crowning of Andros occur and peace negotiations began in earnest.

docs.title: Outcome
docs.text: Normund achieved most of his objectives, with the exception of conquering Enetia and Asparon. The second succession crisis was pre-empted and his rivals quelled by gaining a large stake in the loot. He also achieved his religious objectives, allowing Thalen priests to be recognised by the Sect of Pyos in Movenna.

docs.title: War of Recovery
docs.text: A current war in south-eastern Ceneria and Zinnabar, led by Andros and the Cenerian League against Xeril and his Rurii conquerors.

docs.title: Xeril's Conquests
docs.text:
Conquests of the Rurii warlord Xeril, conquering the cities of Thenea Deuteron, Zeutana, and Saphos.

The cities of Sydon and Myrdenia were heavily sacked but not conquered, remaining part of the Cenerian League.

docs.title: Anchorites
docs.text: The Anchorites are an ascetic group who have mastered the self. They live in isolation, often undertaking pilgrimages of the world to spread the word of the inner self to those who will listen, otherwise spending time alone in caves, huts, and rare 'temples of the self' where they sometimes congregate. Sages of the Seventh Sanctum are known to have come into contact with Anchorites, who already began appearing in Sydon during the Mycean Age. The sages concluded that Anchorites have highly developed Echoes and can will them into action, especially when combined with the use of Arcanum.

docs.title: Beliefs
docs.text: The Anchorites believe that the highest form of existence is in the self. They acknowledge, however, that wisdom not shared is lost; individual masters will teach aspirants who show particular promise in secluded places. They are known to construct shrines and one or two-person shelters in secluded places, with stone markers to guide the way to those who seek it.

docs.title: Kharakt
docs.text: Anchorites use a system of scarring and tattooing known as 'Kharakt'. It is through this system, or at least partially so, that they achieve greater-than-life abilities. Through this skin modification, their body becomes akin to a wizard's scroll, albeit the changes are often permanent. Common modifications include scripts such as 'skin of iron', 'reflexes of the fly', 'speed of the Ryke', and so on.

docs.title: Cenerian League
docs.text:
A present-day incarnation of the First League, albeit at a reduced scale due to the Collapse. It is sometimes called the 'Second League'.

Current members include:

  1. Pallas
  2. Enetia
  3. Lynara
  4. Asparon
  5. Varanth
  6. Naporia
  7. Myrdenia
  8. Seleia
  9. Iphora
  10. Movenna
  11. Aspola
  12. Mara-Halyon
  13. Karsolam
  14. Pylos

Former cities:

  1. Ostellyth
  2. Sydon
  3. Thenea Epeson
  4. Thenea Deuteron
  5. Koray
  6. Oneos
  7. Tanura
  8. Saphos
  9. Zeutana

docs.title: Dunwarden
docs.text: The Dunwarden are an ancient order of rangers which patrol the Akkron Wall. They swear no loyalty to any ruler nor god, and keep mostly to themselves.

docs.title: Origin
docs.text: The etymology of the Dunwarden name suggests a Brethiln origin, and indeed, the Brethiln have a history of an ancient tribe which lived and presumably patrolled the Akkron Wall. History turns to myth here, though there are suggestions that this tribe was eventually hired by the Akkurim to continue their patrols along the mountain range, presumably to defend against threats from the west. After Mycean victory over the Akkurim, said tribe likely switched sides, quite literally, to patrol the same mountains against threats from the east. This, so far, traces the most likely origin.

docs.title: Organisation
docs.text: The Dunwarden are organised around patrols and base camps, the locations of which are known only to them. They have a strong presence in Naporia and Linov, which act as northern and southern axes of organisation and recruitment.

docs.title: Recruitment
docs.text: The Dunwarden are a politically agnostic order, taking in members from any social strata, religion, and creed. Their primary method of recruitment is by offering sanctuary to persecuted peoples, such as criminals and traitors.

docs.title: First League
docs.text: An ancient alliance of over 200 cities prior to the Collapse led by the now-lost city of Mycea.

docs.title: Sect of Illumination
docs.text:
The Sect of Illumination is a dispersed sect which primarily worships Luminor, the god of light, fire, life, and truth, represented by the sun. They are sometimes called Luminari or Luminoi by others, though they refer to themselves (and everyone else) as 'Luminor's children'.

Unlike the Sect of Pyos, the Sect of Illumination has no main temple, though its followers are particularly numerous in Aspola where the dawn is particularly bright.

docs.title: Beliefs
docs.text: The Sect of Illumination are devout followers of the light, burning fires brightly during Erona's pitch black nights to stave off Bel's encroachment. They believe the Collapse was a cyclical event, and that, eventually, all of Erona will be doused in a cleansing fire to rid it of its impurities and turn the world into one ready for Luminor and his devoutest followers to inhabit. For them, the stars during the night are the holes in Bel's veil of darkness, where Luminor attempts to break through. The fact that no holes of darkness exist during daytime confirms for them the belief that the light surpasses the dark. They also believe in Pyos, albeit classify him closer to a demi-god than god proper, raising Luminor as the perfect deity to worship.

docs.title: Role
docs.text: Unlike the Sect of Pyos, the Sect of Illumination has no official role in Cenerian legal codes. They stand in stark contrast to the secretive writings of the Pyosians and their use of Arcanum, instead preferring to swear public oaths in daylight.

docs.title: Organisation
docs.text: The Sect of Illumination is divided into chapters in major cities but has very little hierachy beyond recognising seniority and particular devotion. Much of the sect is grass-roots: every village and town usually has a holy man dedicated to interpreting the signs of Luminor for the sake of weather and harvests. These and their chapterhouse compatriots in the city are often known as 'wakers' for their custom of attending dawn and subsequently waking their community through throat-singing amplified via bodies of water or horns. Some are also specialised as 'seers' who claim to be able to divine the future from fire, which is considered Luminor's presence made manifest. Beyond this are the twelves 'Flames', each of them considered a high priest unto their own. The Flames each run an important temple dedicated to an aspect of Luminor, usually based in a Cenerian city.

docs.title: Magic
docs.text: Unlike the Sect of Pyos, the Sect of Illumination despises Arcanum (which was developed long after sun-worship began in Mycea, making the sect distrustful of it). Instead, the seers are known to be able to divine possible futures contained within fire, and some clerics cut a substance known as 'Sun crystal' into shapes that can perform miraculous things, such as storing light and even focusing that light into powerful rays.

docs.title: Sect of Pyos
docs.text: The Sect of Pyos is a henotheistic cult, believing in the god Pyos above all other gods but begrudgingly acknowledging that they nevertheless exist. Some more extreme cliques of the sect believe that all other deities are false, and that only Pyos exists. The sect's patriarch and main temple are based in Movenna. They are often referred to as Pyosians.

docs.title: Beliefs
docs.text: The Pyosians believe in law, order, and oaths above all else, without which (they argue) the world would fall into further collapse. They blame the Collapse on the devolution of law and order in the First League. The Sect refers to its members as the 'Sworn' after they have taken and signed an oath in Arcanum.

docs.title: Role
docs.text: The Sect is known for its exceptional scribes, who craft texts in Arcanum at various levels of binding. Their interpretation of the law is more or less followed throughout the realms, particularly in Ceneria and Thalendor. Thalendor especially keeps a close eye that its constituents follow this code. Knights of Thalendor who wish to serve the king need to be sworn members of the Sect of Pyos, taking the title of Paladin.

docs.title: Hierarchy
docs.text: The Pyosian Sect is divided into a strict hierarchy of unsworn and sworn, the latter of which can be considered for priesthood, the former only for scribal duty. After attaining priesthood,

docs.title: Seventh Sanctum
docs.text: A refuge for sages, mages, and scholars on a small island chain near Pyrgoa.

docs.title: Sigodo
docs.text:
The Sigodo are an infamous order of assassins that reside within their mountain fortress near Mara-Halyon on Zinnabar. They are prolific users (and importers) of Haze and believe that using it while performing a kill results in entering an enlightened state. They are overt worshippers of Anzo, a fact that makes employing them difficult and has invited nemesis for those patrons that were caught hiring them.

It is rumoured that Andros used the Sigodo's reputation to coerce some Archons into supporting his coronation during the Crowning of Andros.

docs.title: Trading Fellowship
docs.text:
Trading Fellowships are prominent throughout the Cenerian League as merchant unions that use collective bargaining as a tool against the powerful nobles that seek to restrict their wealth and power. To Cenerian society, merchants are a vital but looked-down-upon class, considered greedy, two-faced, and without honour.

Trading Fellowships are unique to each city, but they keep a loose overall connection with one another. A trader from, say, Aspola, will find lodging and contacts in the Trading Fellowship of Essalon despite not being a local member.

The Trading Fellowships also act as a kind of bank, issuing letters of credit instead of sending valuable currency wherever possible. These are usually only accepted between merchants trading with one another, but some artisans and nobles also make use of them.

docs.title: Erona
docs.text:
The Known World. Erona as a world has no moon, and its nights are very dark.

The following continents are known to exist:

Hespenor Osera (including the Karrow, the Unsung Lands and the Far South East) Zinnabar

docs.title: Mycea
docs.text: Tragic Mycea, lost to the waters of the Collapse! Our scrolls tell of golden towers and domes that lined your roofs, and ever was Luminor's favour upon you. First of the First League, last to fall.

docs.title: Meta
docs.text: The now-lost capital of the Mycean people, located on the former continent of Halcyon. Its exact location is unknown, though all sages agree that it lies in the deepest parts of the Sea of Alvos.

docs.title: Oneos
docs.text: A former Cenerian League city sacked, looted, and effectively razed to the ground after the Voyage of Normund. It has now been abandoned by its former Cenerian population.

docs.title: Tanura
docs.text: A former Cenerian League city sacked, looted, and effectively razed to the ground after the Voyage of Normund. It has now been abandoned by its former Cenerian population.

docs.title: Thenea Deuteron
docs.text: Former city of the Cenerian League, conquered by the Rurii during Xeril's Conquests, subsequently becoming their capital until the conquest of Zeutana, where Xeril's court eventually moved. The city now lies abandoned.

docs.title: Thenea Epeson
docs.text: Former city of the Cenerian League which was abandoned after a bright flash engulfed nearly all of the metropolis. Only fishermen and merchant ships survived the ensuing blast. Rumoured to have left behind a crater with perfectly smooth, mirror-like walls.

docs.title: Essalon
docs.text: Former Cenerian League city now controlled by a vassal Archon nominally loyal to Thalendor.

docs.title: Koray
docs.text: Formerly known as Qorai, Koray was looted during the Voyage of Normund and left to Brethiln of Morenia to inhabit. Most of its Cenerian colonists fled or began accommodating their new Brethiln overlords. Nevertheless, Koray has always had great Brethiln influences, and its new rulers had a de jure presence in the city for centuries.

docs.title: Ostellyth
docs.text:
Formerly Osellos, Ostellyth is the capital of the kingdom of Thalendor and the largest city in northern Osera.

It is ruled by the Aldrician dynasty, founded by the great king Aldric who led his people from the Unsung Lands to Osera.

The Voyage of Normund was begun from Ostellyth, as Normund was able to use the powerful ships captured during the city's siege to launch his invasions.

docs.title: - Grain capital of the North with appropriate harbour and merchant fleet to accompany it (mostly taken over by the Thalen after its conquest with minimal disruptions)
Saphos
docs.text: Former city of the Cenerian League located on Zinnabar, conquered by the Rurii during Xeril's Conquests.

docs.title: Zeutana
docs.text: Former city of the Cenerian League, located on Zinnabar, conquered by the Rurii during Xeril's Conquests, eventually becoming their capital.

docs.title: Bal Gorund
docs.text:
A city under the mountain, Bal-Gorund is the last refuge of the Manlings, who have made it their impregnable fortress. No outsiders are allowed in or out, under penalty of death.

Bal-Gorund is located in the highest peak of the Akkron Wall and is known for its labyrinthine tunnels and cave systems. Those who have never been speak of grand underground halls, those who claim to have escaped it speak only of cramped and dark tunnels.

docs.title: Linov
docs.text: Linov is the capital of Lonovia. Though often described as a city, Linov is small compared by Cenerian standards and more akin to a heavily fortified town. It is one of three Brethiln 'cities', the other two being Koray and Vorne.

docs.title: Sydon
docs.text: Former city of the Cenerian League, sacked during Xeril's Conquests and subsequently became independent. Home of piracy and links to the Far South East. It is from here that the Anchorites first spread into Osera.

docs.title: Vorne
docs.text: Vorne is the capital of the Brethiln realm of Velwynne. It is one of three Brethiln 'cities', the other two being Koray and Linov.

docs.title: Asparon
docs.text: A member of the Cenerian League.

docs.title: Aspola
docs.text: Last remaining Cenerian League aligned city on Zinnabar, and the first founded there by the Myceans. Its inhabitants are nominally Cenerian but often still refer to themselves as Mycean.

docs.title: Enetia
docs.text: Largest city in the Cenerian north of Osera and owner of the second-largest navy in the Cenerian League (the first being Pallas), known for its successful defence during the Voyage of Normund, holding out over several years.

docs.title: Founding
docs.text: Enetia was founded as a small trading outpost during the Mycean Age, intended to trade along what was then a large river flowing into Halcyon. After the flood, Enetians had to confront their new oceanic environment which now surrounded them on all sides. Their new lagoon allowed them to build a flourishing port city, safe from the Horrors of the mainland.

docs.title: Iphora
docs.text: A member of the Cenerian League.

docs.title: Karsolam
docs.text: Last-of-its-kind fortress city on the doomed continent of Hespenor, Karsolam is both a refuge and a prison; its walls protect from the Horrors on the outside but keep its indentured population in place. Karsolam is a member of the Cenerian League. It is the only Mycean city with a majority population of Qedhi.

docs.title: Lynara
docs.text: A member of the Cenerian League.

docs.title: Mara Halyon
docs.text: A member of the Cenerian League. Known for its nearby Sigodo fortress monastery.

docs.title: Movenna
docs.text: A member of the Cenerian League. Home to the Sect of Pyos, its patriarch, and its main temple complex. Large influx of devout Thalen migrants, which has resulted in tensions in the overcrowded city.

docs.title: Myrdenia
docs.text:
Capital city of the island Misoros. Sacked during Xeril's Conquests but remained part of the Cenerian League, albeit in diminished form.

Myrdenia's former wealth was largely made up from its vast salt mines located on Misoros.

docs.title: Naporia
docs.text: A fortress-city in the south-east of Ceneria, and a member of the Cenerian League. Known for resisting Xeril's Conquests and controlling the relatively prosperous Naporian Realm.

docs.title: Pallas
docs.text: De-facto leader of the Cenerian League and based on the island of Pyrgoa.

docs.title: Pylos
docs.text: A member of the Cenerian League.

docs.title: Seleia
docs.text: A member of the Cenerian League and located on the island of Pyrgoa.

docs.title: Varanth
docs.text:
A city in the Valkyros region which has sworn nominal allegiance to the Cenerian League. However, the city is internally divided. On its throne sits 'Arkon' Grag, an Altani mercenary-turned-conqueror who dethroned the previous Archon Enkono after he refused to pay Grag's warband. Further, a group of Thalen soldiers and their families have settled across the river after a failed siege (foiled by Grag's warband). They have now come to a truce with Grag as the new ruler, and have begun turning their siege camp into a makeshift settlement called Ondervar (lit. 'Other/New Var{anth}).

Grag continues to pay tribute to Andros and the Cenerian League, but Cenerian honour demands a response, with many speculating that Pallas and Enetia will soon send a force to take back the city. This, however,

docs.title: Pillars of Bel
docs.text: In the far south-western seas, the Altom Ocean lie the pillars of Bel. If the tall tales spun by sailors are to be believed, the Pillars of Bel are vast waterfalls cast from high in the heavens, rushing down into the ocean from unknowable heights. They are believed to be Bel's connection to his watery domain from the heavens where he resides, and some swear they have seen the god travel within those watery roads.

docs.title: Sigodo Fortress Monastery
docs.text:
Equal parts mountain hideout and fortress monastery, the Sigodo have a long tradition of residence here. First established by outcasts from Aspola for overtly worshipping Anzo, the initial village eventually grew beyond its capacity to maintain its population, probably resulting in the founding of Mara-Halyon, though this is disputed.

In any case, the location quickly became depopulated and a hardening of religious views began. This eventually culminated in hostility between the rulers of Mara-Halyon and the Sigodo, necessitating the construction of fortifications. The eventual and suspicious demise of Mara-Halyon's Archon, however, meant that open hostilities never broke out.

docs.title: Underworld
docs.text:
The Underworld is the supposed place where souls are judged by Mor's judges. It is mostly believed to exist by the citizens of Karsolam, who worship Mor out of fear. It is a relatively obscure term in Osera and Zinnabar, though some death cults there are known to exist.

For their part, worshippers of Pyos and Luminor believe that the Underworld is only entered if the dead body isn't burnt, and thus would not allow the soul (in forms of ashes) to rise to the heavens. The people of Karsolam believe that no matter the funeral method, all souls need to march west to the gates of the Underworld in the Lands of Mor, ideally found and guided by the Pale Maiden.

docs.title: Brethil
docs.text:
A shrinking realm, Brethil used to cover the central and northern portions of Osera, before the conquering Thalen established the great realm of Thalendor in the centre of Osera. Brethil is predominantly inhabited by the Brethiln people, who have, however, slowly been replaced by the Thalen and Altani.

Brethil is subdivided into three smaller realms: Velwynne, Morenia, and Lonovia.

docs.title: Ceneria
docs.text:
The southern realm of Osera, inhabited by the Cenerian people. Ceneria is a wider realm divided into smaller realms, each centred around an initial Mycean colony. As such, the realms themselves are less important than their capitals, which command most the population within their walls.

The Cenerian realms and their associated cities are:

  • Valkyros - Varanth
  • Lithandor - Movenna

Islands associated with Ceneria: Pyrgoa - Pallas & Seleia Rialta - Enetia Misoros - Myrdenia Gorgos - Essalon & Halessa (abandoned) Mycorai - Pylos (abandoned) Valia - Thenea Epeson (abandoned)

docs.title: Lonovia
docs.text: Lonovia is one of three remaining Brethiln realms in northern Osera, the other two being Morenia and Velwynne. Its capital is Linov.

docs.title: Morenia
docs.text: Morenia is one of three remaining Brethiln realms in northern Osera, the other two being Velwynne and Lonovia. Its capital is Koray.

docs.title: Naporian Realm
docs.text: A relatively large and prosperous realm controlled by Naporia, lying mostly within the Valley of Plenty and on the southern Cenerian Peaks.

docs.title: Thalendor
docs.text: Kingdom of the Thalen, ruled by the Aldrician dynasty. Capital is Ostellyth.

docs.title: Velwynne
docs.text: Velwynne is one of three remaining Brethiln realms in northern Osera, the other two being Morenia and Lonovia. Its capital is Vorne.

docs.title: Akkron Wall
docs.text:
The Akkron Wall, sometimes simply called the 'Akkra', is the main dividing mountain range between Osera and the Karrow as well as the rest of the east, such as the Unsung Lands.

It was first discovered by Mycean explorers and colonists, who in turn met the Akkurim which inhabited the range and the lands now known as the Karrow there. Today, the Akkron Wall is the primary defence against creatures from the eastern wastelands and is patrolled by the Dunwarden.

The Wall is home to the Manlings city of Bal-Gorund, as well as several Altani encampments. Mountain Men also inhabit the range, as well as some Skrats. Further, the finest and largest Rykes make their nests here.

docs.title: Far South East
docs.text:
A relatively unknown region whose merchants and pirates sometimes visit Sydon, which has become a port of convenience. Some of these have struck out further from the Sydonese Archipelago and have even clashed with Rurii and Cenerian ships.

Not much is known about the mysterious Far South East, sometimes called ... All that is known is learned from their merchants and pirates who are known to tell tall tales. Nevertheless, some basic facts have been constructed by the Sanctum, mainly that an offshoot of the Rurii has been terrorising those realms as well, though with less success than Xeril's Conquests. We also know that

docs.title: Halcyon
docs.text: The home continent of the First League prior to the Collapse. Now split into Osera in the East and Hespenor in the West.

docs.title: Hespenor
docs.text:
Often nicknamed the 'Far Land', Hespenor is the western remnant of Halcyon. It suffered greatly after the Collapse, with only one surviving city from the First League, the fortified city of Karsolam.

Home to the lands of Mor, the Sea of Nightmares and the Forest of Glass.

docs.title: Karrow
docs.text:
The Karrow, or Karrowland, is a wasteland east of Osera. Its wastes give way to dry steppes and mesas, and only a few rivers. The Rurii called the Karrow their home before many migrated after Xeril's Conquests.

The Karrow is permanently steeped in low-lying Haze, which rises depending on weather and season. For this reason, Horrors regularly stalk the wastes.

docs.title: Lands of Mor
docs.text:
The Lands of Mor is the region that surrounds Karsolam in Hespenor and is named after the death deity of Mor. It is allegedly the location of the gates to the Underworld.

The Lands of Mor are extremely dangerous, known for their rich presence of Haze and thus Horrors. Volcanic activity and earthquakes are also common, splitting the grey landscape apart with ravines, canyons, and occasional rivers of magma.

docs.title: Osera
docs.text:
The Eastern continent of Erona. It is marked by tall mountain ranges and few plains, though great rivers let what little cultivatable land there is quite fertile.

Home to three cultures, Cenerian, Brethiln, and the Thalen.

Contiguous with the Unsung Lands but split by a large prairie dotted by mesas.

docs.title: Unsung Lands
docs.text: The region east of Osera. The two are technically connected by an unfertile prairie dotted with mesas but are usually spoken of as two separate lands.

docs.title: Zinnabar
docs.text:
A dry and windswept continent south of Osera and the Sea of Azuth.

Home to three League cities:

docs.title: Barrier Islands
docs.text: A chain of islands in the Sea of Alvos, formerly the highest peaks in Halcyon and spared the deluge during the Collapse. The 2nd, 3rd, and 4th Sanctums were built here when their sages correctly predicted the Collapse. Some temples and vaults are rumoured to exist here, too, containing treasures beyond imagining according to folklore.

docs.title: Gorgos
docs.text: A large island off the western coast of Osera and one of the first to be colonised by the Myceans, now a firm part of Cenerian culture.

docs.title: Kaphar
docs.text: An island closely located to western Ceneria, connected to the mainland via one of the bridges believed to have been built by the Titans.

docs.title: Misoros
docs.text: An island off the southern coast of Ceneria and home to the city of Myrdenia. Known for its extensive salt deposits and mines.

docs.title: Mycorai
docs.text: An island off the coast of Zinnabar, with the Sea of Azuth on its eastern and Sea of Alvos on its western side, respectively. It is considered part of the wider realm of Ceneria. The abandoned city of Pylos, which was founded by Mycean colonists centuries ago, is located here.

docs.title: Pyrgoa
docs.text: A large island lying off the south of Osera and north of Zinnabar. It is home to the Cenerian League city (and de facto leader) of Pallas.

docs.title: Rialta
docs.text: An island off the coast of Thalendor, though traditionally associated with the Cenerian realm. It is the location of Enetia, one of the leading cities of the Cenerian League.

docs.title: Sydonese Archipelago
docs.text: The labyrinthine expanse of islands that must be navigate to reach Sydon.

docs.title: The Eye
docs.text: A massive impact crater in the Sea of Alvos between Osera and Hespenor in what used to be northern Halcyon.

docs.title: Origin
docs.text: The Eye was created when the largest shard of the falling star that caused the Collapse impacted northern Halcyon. The sages of the Seventh Sanctum are still stupefied by the sheer size of the crater, calculating that, at its size, nothing should have remained of Erona at all.

docs.title: Resources
docs.text: The Eye is the single greatest source of concentrated Haze which can best be brewed into the well-known street drug known by the same name. Enetia has an effective monopoly over the region, but has also begun exploring Hespenor's craters for similar concentrations with some success.

docs.title: Valia
docs.text: The island of the doomed and abandoned city of Thenea Epeson. Its sister island, Deinos, is home to the largest active volcano on Osera.

docs.title: Altom Ocean
docs.text:
The Altom Ocean lies between the respective southern and western tips of Hespenor and Zinnabar. It is a vast ocean that has never been explored by Myceans or Cenerians, their ships (built for the calm, northern seas) toppling too easily in the high waves.

The Pillars of Bel are said to be found if one dares go far enough, but few have seen them, and even fewer have returned.

docs.title: Sea of Alvos
docs.text: The sea forming the new divide between Osera and Hespenor after the central part of Halcyon experienced near-endless flooding. The Sea of Alvos is named after the Sovereign of the Mycean people who ruled during the Collapse. The sea itself is quite shallow, particularly in formerly hilly and mountainous areas, requiring deceptive amount of skill to navigate. Those now often form island chains, still named after their former peaks.

docs.title: Sea of Azuth
docs.text: The sea that divides Osera from Zinnabar. The islands of Pyrgoa and Gorgos lie within it. It is named after the infamous founder of the Sixth Sanctum before it was destroyed and subsequently moved to its current iteration at the Seventh Sanctum.

docs.title: Arcanum
docs.text:
The following is drawn from "On the Nature and Degrees of Arcanum," a foundational text of the Seventh Sanctum, revised in its current form in the 143rd year of the Cenerian Age.

Arcanum is the written language shared by the Mycean and Cenerian peoples, and is, to the best of our knowledge, the most powerful instrument of magic available to mortal hands. It is the position of the Seventh Sanctum that Arcanum does not merely describe reality but, under sufficient conditions, actively shapes it by working through the medium of Echoes to impose the written word upon the world.

We do not use the word "magic" lightly. The Sanctum has spent centuries cataloguing the precise conditions under which written Arcanum produces measurable effects, and the results are unambiguous. What follows is the framework our predecessors developed to describe Arcanum's five ascending degrees of power. It remains the most useful taxonomy we have, though we acknowledge that we can only say what each degree does far more confidently than we can say why.

docs.title: The Five Degrees
docs.text:
The first degree is the recording of the already-true such as a ledger of grain in the granary, a deed for land in lawful possession, or a merchant's stock. At this degree, Arcanum exerts no real force and is merely a precise language.

The second degree, witnessing the agreed, is the first point at which Arcanum begins to act upon the world: a contract between consenting parties, a judgment where facts are not in dispute. Here Arcanum does not create truth so much as fix it, making what was agreed difficult to unmake.

The third degree is imposing the disputed and is where the more cautious among us counsel restraint. A binding placed upon someone who has not consented, a judgment rendered over protest are where Arcanum exerts magical force against mortal resistance. Its effects have been documented extensively.

The fourth degree, establishing the new, has been achieved by untrained hands only a handful of times in recorded history. A law with no prior existence, a boundary with no prior agreement, a truth the world had not previously acknowledged are possible but require an exceptional writer, exceptional conditions, and, we suspect, something we do not yet understand about the relationship between the writer and their Echo. This degree is what the Sanctum hopes to achieve in all its aspirants.

The fifth degree — altering the world itself — is extreme. Moving mountains. Redirecting rivers. Raising the dead. The Sanctum itself has only produced one known candidate of this nature who did not go insane, Azuth himself. All others that were known were outcast or executed for their insanity.

docs.title: On the Sect of Pyos
docs.text: The Sect of Pyos holds that Arcanum was given to humanity by Pyos himself at the founding of Mycea, and that it is therefore sacred as the very voice of the god, which is why oaths sworn in Arcanum carry such weight. The Sanctum neither endorses nor refutes this claim. What we observe is that Arcanum functions consistently regardless of whether the writer holds any particular religious view, which suggests its power is a property of the language itself rather than divine favour. We leave the theological question to others.

docs.title: On the Sect of Illumination
docs.text: The Sect of Illumination mistrusts Arcanum and written language in general, preferring to preserve knowledge orally and through song. We note that oral traditions are considerably more vulnerable to drift, misremembering, and deliberate alteration than written ones, and that the Sanctum's archives have preserved knowledge the Illumination's oral tradition has long since lost.

docs.title: Geomancery
docs.text:
A note from the Seventh Sanctum's comparative catalogue of magical traditions, compiled in the 137th year of the Cenerian Age.

Geomancery is, as best we can determine, the oldest magical tradition in the known world — predating Arcanum, Phasonomy, and any tradition we can trace through written record. We know this primarily because evidence of Geomancery has been found at sites we associate with the Titans, which places its origins well before the Mycean Age and possibly before the Fabled Age as well. What the Titans understood about it, we have inferred with some but not complete confidence.

The practice, as it survives today, involves carving specific shapes and symbols into natural materials such as earth, stone, wood, to produce magical effects. The underlying mechanism appears related to the same principles as Arcanum: inscription imposing intention upon the world. Whether Geomancery and Arcanum share a common origin, or whether Arcanum was developed independently and happened to converge on similar principles, is a question the Sanctum has debated for generations without resolution.

Our knowledge of Geomancery is frustratingly thin. Living practitioners are rare, and those we have encountered have been reluctant to share the details of their craft. The carvings we have studied in the field are difficult to interpret without a practitioner's guidance. We suspect that significant knowledge has been lost, and that what survives is a fragment of what was once a far more developed tradition. If any scholar has access to a willing practitioner, the Sanctum would consider this a matter of considerable priority.

One area of Geomancery that survives is the Druidry of the Brethiln, which combines shape carving of natural materials with potion brewing in inscribed cauldrons. Theirs, however, is a secretive tradition and make a point of refusing to teach outsiders. It is the Sanctum's goal to invite a delegation of druids to record their ways before they are lost to time.

docs.title: Kharakt
docs.text:
A note from the Seventh Sanctum's comparative catalogue of magical traditions, compiled in the 137th year of the Cenerian Age.

'Kharakt' is our best approximation of the Anchorites' own word for their practice as the Cenerian tongue has no native equivalent. It refers to the ritual scarring, and in some cases tattooing, by which Anchorite practitioners inscribe their bodies in order to compel their Echoes to perform magic. The results, as attested by those Sanctum scholars who have observed Anchorites in the field, are remarkable.

The obvious parallel to Arcanum is not lost on us. Both traditions involve inscription as a means of compelling an Echo to act. Arcanum works in ink on parchment, Kharakt in scar tissue on skin. Whether this difference is merely practical or reflects some deeper divergence in how each tradition understands the relationship between inscription and Echo, we cannot say as the Anchorites have not been forthcoming on the subject, and our Cenerian concepts may simply be inadequate to describe what they are doing.

What is clear is that Kharakt has different effects from Arcanum. While Arcanum can theoretically produce any outcome imaginable, Kharakt is limited to amplifying feats of the human body. Nevertheless, Kharakt has upsides: it seems less taxing on its user (and their Echo), and it does not need to be studied (the magic is applied when inscribed and remains permanent afterwards).

A further limitation of Kharakt is that the body is a finite canvas. A practitioner can only bear so many inscriptions, which places a limit on the power available to any individual. Whether this constraint can be worked around through finer inscription, or through covering a larger surface area, or through some method we have not yet observed, is unknown to us. We would welcome correspondence from any scholar with closer access to Anchorite practice.

docs.title: Phasonomy
docs.text:
A note from the Seventh Sanctum's comparative catalogue of magical traditions, compiled in the 137th year of the Cenerian Age.

Phasonomy is the oldest magical tradition native to the Mycean and Cenerian cultures, though we must be precise here, because it is not the oldest tradition we are aware of overall. That distinction belongs to Geomancery, which predates Phasonomy by an unknown but significant age, likely even to the Age of Titans. Phasonomy, on the other hand, was developed some time before the Mycean Age, when Glimstone was first cut into specific shapes. The Sect of Illumination is the only organisation known to the Sanctum that is able to wield this power, though we have had some success in our own experiments too.

Phasonomy is nearly unthinkable without Glimstone. It requires precise cutting, shaping, and wielding in order to achieve anything close to what we might describe as 'magic'. Different levels of precision can achieve different performances:

A basic level of Phasonomy will allow the wielder to cut a stone that can store and emit light, a relatively simple technique but unattainable without the right know-how. The Luminari use this in everything from walking staves to interior lighting.

At a more advanced level, Phasonomy allows the wielder to channel stored light into more and more destructive beams, often achieved with lenses of Glimstones to achieve even higher concentrations of light. An advanced user can even achieve powerful fireballs and explosions in this manner.

At the highest levels, a wielder of Phasonomy can be expected to briefly turn night to day, and to channel stored light within Glimstone in far away places. This requires extreme geometries of Glimstone that has not yet been reproduced at the Sanctum. It is further unclear to us how light from beyond a wielder's own region is channelled, though we do know that there is some delay at far enough distances.

docs.title: A Noble's Slice of Life
docs.text:
Lucon, 25 year-old son of Semnon, wakes in the Gold's Landing district of Enetia to the sound of the Sect of Illumination's call of dawn, amplified by large horns that bathe the city in the guttural sound of their throat singing.
